text: The surgeon begins dissection by incising the medial peritoneal fold near the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), developing a plane beneath the IMV. A vascular plane is created close to the mesocolic fascia, proceeding medially to laterally. The IMV is dissected up to the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure, followed by central venous ligation and high clipping of the IMV. Further dissection exposes the pancreas superiorly and enters the lesser sac. The sigmoid colon is lifted to make the groove prominent, and the peritoneum is incised over it. The dissection plane is close to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), sparing nerves. The left ureter is identified and preserved. Dissection continues towards the IMA route, with nerves preserved and the IMA route skeletonized.

The surgeon performs high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) with clipping at its origin. Medial to lateral dissection is carried out in the avascular plane towards the lateral abdominal wall. The lateral peritoneal attachment is incised to mobilize the descending and sigmoid colon. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized by cutting the lateral peritoneal attachment to gain extra length for colorectal anastomosis. Posterior dissection continues close to the mesorectal fascia, with visible preservation of nerves.

The surgeon identifies the right hypogastric nerve during posterior dissection. The dissection plane is altered to excise the presacral fascia, revealing the presacral venous flexure. Care is taken to prevent injury and bleeding in this area.

The surgeon takes a vascular approach on the left side due to extra major rectal disease. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is incised, extending the peritoneal cut along the external iliac vessels. The iliac vessels are exposed, and the obliterated umbilical artery is resected. The left ureter is carefully resected and moved laterally away from the resection site. Dissection continues along the left internal iliac vessel.

The surgeon sacrifices the nerves on the left side and removes the entire tissue from the lateral pelvic wall along with the pre-sacral fascia as part of the specimen. The anterior peritoneal fold is incised, and a dissection plane is developed between the rectum and the vagina. The rectum is mobilized on the right lateral pelvic side while preserving the nerve.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, cutting the rectovaginal septum to facilitate further mobilization of the rectal tube. Complete circumferential mobilization of the rectum is achieved. The specimen is divided with an endo stapler and extracted through the ileostomy site. An intracorporeal stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler, followed by the creation of a diversion loop ileostomy.
